title: Developmental psychology: developing an identity
summary: Identity is how a person answers 'Who am I?' — and psychology treats it as something built, not born. This lesson separates personal identity from social identity, then works through how gender identity and gender roles develop via three competing explanations: biological (prenatal hormones), cognitive (gender-schema theory), and social (social learning). It links each explanation to a named supporting study used the way examiners want, sets it inside the social/cultural context and peer influence, adds the adolescence angle (Erikson and Marcia in brief), and builds towards a full Paper 2 essay marked against the real IB markband out of 22.
learningObjectives: Distinguish personal identity from social identity, and gender identity from gender roles.
Explain and contrast biological, cognitive (gender-schema), and social (social learning) explanations of how gender identity and roles develop.
Use named supporting studies (aim, procedure, findings) to support — not just decorate — an argument.
Evaluate the explanations and research, including reductionism, cultural bias, and methodology.
Plan and write a Paper 2 essay marked against the IB markband out of 22, and know the caps that keep answers out of the top bands.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Who Am I — and Who Told Me So?
simpleExplanation.summary: Your identity is your sense of who you are: partly the private, personal traits you feel define you, and partly the social groups you belong to. A big piece of it — your sense of being a boy, a girl, both, or neither, and how you're 'meant' to behave because of it — is built from three ingredients working at once: your biology, the way your mind sorts the world into categories, and the people who reward, punish, and model behaviour around you.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of identity like a playlist. Some tracks were pre-loaded before you could choose (biology). Some you added because you learned the 'rules' of what fits your vibe and filtered out anything that clashed (your mental schema). And a lot got added because friends played them, you saw them liked, and you copied (social learning). The final playlist feels totally 'you' — but almost every track has a source you didn't invent alone.
steps: Separate personal identity (my own traits and values) from social identity (the groups I belong to).
Separate gender identity (my internal sense of my gender) from gender role (the behaviours a society expects from that gender).
Line up the three explanations of gender development: biological, cognitive/gender-schema, and social learning — and see what each can and cannot explain.
Put it in context: culture and peers shape which behaviours get modelled and reinforced.
Practise turning all of this into a marked Paper 2 essay out of 22.

flashcards.back: The part of the self-concept built from individual attributes — traits, abilities, values and personal history — that a person feels makes them a unique individual, distinct from others.

The part of the self-concept derived from membership of social groups (e.g. nationality, religion, gender, sports team). Tajfel's social identity theory argues we categorise ourselves into groups and take part of our self-esteem from those group memberships.

Gender IDENTITY is a person's internal, private sense of their own gender. Gender ROLE is the set of behaviours, attitudes and activities a society treats as appropriate for that gender. Identity is felt; role is performed and culturally defined.

Gender development is influenced by biological factors — especially prenatal hormones (androgens such as testosterone) that organise the developing brain, alongside genes and chromosomes. Predicts a partly innate basis for gender-typed behaviour.

Bem (1981) and Martin & Halverson (1981): once children can label their own gender, they build a schema — an organised set of beliefs about what each gender does. They then actively attend to, remember and imitate schema-consistent information and filter out inconsistent information.

Bandura: gender behaviour is learned through observation, imitation of same-sex models, and differential reinforcement (rewarding gender-'appropriate' behaviour, discouraging the rest). Vicarious reinforcement means children copy behaviour they see rewarded in others.

Girls with congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH), exposed to high prenatal androgens, showed greater preference for boys' toys than unaffected female relatives — evidence that prenatal hormones influence gender-typed behaviour.

Children shown pictures of people in gender-consistent vs gender-inconsistent activities distorted their memory a week later, 'correcting' inconsistent images to fit their schema (e.g. recalling a girl, not a boy, cooking) — support for gender-schema theory.

Observations of parents with toddlers showed differential reinforcement: parents reacted more positively to gender-'appropriate' play, supporting the social-learning account of how gender roles are shaped early.

flashcards.front: Imperato-McGinley (1974) — a caution for social accounts
flashcards.back: In a Dominican community, children with 5-alpha-reductase deficiency were raised as girls but developed male genitalia at puberty and largely adopted a male gender role — used to argue biology can override rearing, complicating purely social explanations.

Erikson: adolescence is the 'identity vs role confusion' stage. Marcia added four statuses based on exploration and commitment (diffusion, foreclosure, moratorium, achievement). Useful as the developmental backdrop to how gender and other identities are consolidated in the teenage years.

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: describe vs USE research
flashcards.back: Retelling a study earns knowledge marks but NOT 'use of research' marks. Research is only 'used' when you explicitly state what it demonstrates about the explanation — e.g. 'this supports the biological account because...'.

quickCheck.prompt: A student writes that a boy's 'internal sense of being a boy' is his gender ROLE. This is wrong because gender role refers to:
options: The chromosomes a person is born with
The society-defined behaviours expected of a gender
The private feeling of being a particular gender
The hormones released before birth
quickCheck.answer: The society-defined behaviours expected of a gender

quickCheck.prompt: According to gender-schema theory, once a child has a gender schema they will tend to:
options: Remember information that contradicts the schema more accurately
Attend to and remember schema-consistent information and distort inconsistent information
Ignore their own gender when choosing activities
Only learn gender behaviour through reinforcement, not thinking
quickCheck.answer: Attend to and remember schema-consistent information and distort inconsistent information

quickCheck.prompt: The social learning explanation predicts a child is MOST likely to imitate a behaviour when they:
options: Are biologically predisposed to it
See a same-sex model rewarded for it
Have reached gender constancy
Have high prenatal androgen exposure
quickCheck.answer: See a same-sex model rewarded for it

quickCheck.prompt: A candidate spends a paragraph retelling the procedure and findings of Berenbaum & Hines (1992) but never says what it shows about the biological explanation. In the IB essay, this mainly costs marks under:
options: Criterion A (focus) only
Criterion C (use of research) — the study is described, not USED
Criterion E (clarity) only
No criterion — describing a study is always enough
quickCheck.answer: Criterion C (use of research) — the study is described, not USED

quickCheck.prompt: Which is the clearest example of SOCIAL identity rather than personal identity?
options: 'I am patient and curious'
'I value honesty above all'
'I am a member of the national swim team'
'I enjoy solving puzzles alone'
quickCheck.answer: 'I am a member of the national swim team'

faq.q: Do I have to argue that ONE explanation of gender is correct?
faq.a: No. The command 'discuss' rewards weighing explanations against each other, not crowning a winner. The strongest essays argue that biological, cognitive and social factors interact — for example, prenatal hormones may bias early preferences, which schemas then organise and social reinforcement then amplifies. A clear interactionist thesis is more sophisticated than picking a single 'right' answer.

faq.q: Is 'gender identity or role' one topic or two?
faq.a: The syllabus content 'gender identity and gender roles' is treated as one connected area, and Paper 2 questions typically write 'gender identity or role'. You can focus your essay on either identity or role, or both, as long as your studies and explanations genuinely match what you claim to be discussing. Don't switch silently between the two — define which you are addressing.

faq.q: How many studies do I need for a top-band essay?
faq.a: Quality beats quantity, but our marking engine caps 'use of research' if only one study is present. Two studies, each thoroughly explained and explicitly tied to an explanation, is the reliable route to the top of Criterion C. Adding a third weakly-explained study rarely helps and eats your time.

sections.content: Developmental psychology asks how we become who we are, and few questions are more central than 'Who am I?'. This lesson treats identity as something constructed over time. We first split identity into its personal and social sides, then zoom in on the part of identity the IB syllabus asks you to explain in most depth — gender identity and gender roles — using three competing explanations, named studies, and the cultural and peer context that shapes them all. Everything builds towards a Paper 2 essay you can write and have marked against the real IB criteria out of 22.
What 'identity' means: personal and social

Identity is a person's sense of who they are — coherent, relatively stable, and used to guide decisions. Psychologists usually divide the self-concept into two strands. Personal identity is built from individual attributes: your traits, abilities, values and personal history — the things that make you feel like a unique individual. Social identity, by contrast, comes from the groups you belong to: your nationality, religion, gender, friendship group or team. Tajfel's social identity theory argues that we categorise ourselves into such groups and draw part of our self-esteem from them, which is why group membership can feel so personal.

items: **Personal identity** — 'I am patient, I value fairness, I love music': individual traits and values.
**Social identity** — 'I am British, a Muslim, a footballer': identity derived from group membership.
The two interact: how much a group matters to you becomes part of who you personally feel you are.
Gender sits at the intersection — it is both a deeply personal sense of self and a powerful social category.

sections.content: Gender identity vs gender role — get the distinction right

Two terms are constantly confused, and mixing them up quietly damages an essay. Gender identity is a person's internal, private sense of their own gender — something felt. Gender role is the set of behaviours, attitudes and activities that a particular society treats as appropriate for that gender — something performed and culturally defined. A person's felt identity and the role their culture assigns can align or clash. Because roles are cultural, they vary across time and place, whereas the experience of having a gender identity appears near-universal even where its content differs.

items: **Gender identity** = the internal sense of being male, female, both or neither.
**Gender role** = the socially expected behaviours attached to a gender ('boys play rough', 'girls nurture').
Roles are culturally variable and historically changeable; identity is felt and comparatively stable.
In an essay, state which one you are discussing and keep your studies matched to it.

sections.content: Explanation 1 — Biological: prenatal hormones organise gender

The biological explanation argues that gender-typed behaviour is influenced by biology, especially prenatal hormones. During foetal development, androgens (such as testosterone) are thought to 'organise' the developing brain in ways that later bias interests, play preferences and some aspects of gender-typed behaviour. This is not a claim that culture is irrelevant, but that there is a partly innate biological push that other factors then build on.

**Supporting study — Berenbaum & Hines (1992).** *Aim:* to test whether atypical prenatal hormone exposure affects gender-typed play. *Procedure:* they studied girls with congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH), a condition that exposes the foetus to unusually high androgen levels, and compared their toy choices with those of unaffected female relatives during a free-play period. *Findings:* CAH girls spent more time with toys typically preferred by boys (e.g. vehicles, construction) than the comparison girls. *Use:* this supports the biological explanation because a difference in prenatal hormones is linked to a difference in gender-typed behaviour, holding much of the family environment constant.

**A second biological line — Imperato-McGinley (1974).** In a Dominican community, some children with 5-alpha-reductase deficiency had ambiguous genitalia and were raised as girls, yet developed male genitalia at puberty and largely adopted a male gender role. This is used to argue that biology can, in some cases, override the gender of rearing — a serious challenge to purely social accounts.
Explanation 2 — Cognitive: gender-schema theory

The cognitive approach argues that children are active thinkers who build gender for themselves. Kohlberg proposed that understanding of gender develops in stages, culminating in gender constancy (knowing gender is fixed). Gender-schema theory (Bem, 1981; Martin & Halverson, 1981) goes further: as soon as a child can reliably label their own gender, they form a schema — an organised set of beliefs about what each gender does. That schema then acts as a filter: children pay more attention to, remember better, and prefer to imitate information that is consistent with their gender, while discounting inconsistent information. Crucially, this can happen earlier than full gender constancy.

**Supporting study — Martin & Halverson (1983).** *Aim:* to test whether children's gender schemas distort memory. *Procedure:* children were shown pictures of people performing activities that were either schema-consistent (e.g. a girl cooking) or schema-inconsistent (e.g. a boy cooking); a week later their memory of the pictures was tested. *Findings:* children more accurately remembered schema-consistent images, and systematically distorted inconsistent ones — for example 'correcting' a boy cooking to a girl cooking. *Use:* this supports gender-schema theory because it shows children actively reshape incoming information to fit an existing gender schema, exactly as the theory predicts — evidence of an internal cognitive process, not just imitation.
Explanation 3 — Social: social learning and reinforcement

The social learning explanation (Bandura) argues that gender roles are learned from the social environment through observation, imitation and reinforcement. Children observe models — especially same-sex models such as parents, peers and media figures — and are more likely to imitate behaviours they see rewarded (vicarious reinforcement). Adults also apply differential reinforcement, responding more warmly to gender-'appropriate' behaviour and discouraging the rest, so children learn which behaviours 'belong' to their gender.

**Supporting study — Fagot (1978).** *Aim:* to see whether parents reinforce gender-typed behaviour in toddlers. *Procedure:* naturalistic observation of parents interacting with their toddlers at home, recording reactions to different kinds of play. *Findings:* parents responded more positively when children engaged in gender-'appropriate' play and more negatively to gender-'inappropriate' play, and this differential reinforcement was present very early. *Use:* this supports the social learning explanation by showing the reinforcement mechanism operating in real family life, shaping gender roles before children could fully articulate them.

sections.question: Worked example 1 — Choosing and USING two studies for the SAME question. You must write on 'gender identity or role'. You have four studies (Berenbaum & Hines 1992, Martin & Halverson 1983, Fagot 1978, Imperato-McGinley 1974). For an essay contrasting the BIOLOGICAL and COGNITIVE explanations, which two studies do you make central, and how do you USE (not just describe) each?
sections.solution:
**Pick the two that map cleanly onto your two chosen explanations:**
- Biological explanation → **Berenbaum & Hines (1992)**.
- Cognitive/gender-schema explanation → **Martin & Halverson (1983)**.

(Fagot and Imperato-McGinley are held in reserve as critical-thinking material, not as your two central studies, because they belong to the social and biological accounts respectively.)

**Turn each from 'described' into 'used' with one linking sentence:**
- After Berenbaum & Hines: *'This is USED as support for the biological explanation because a purely prenatal difference — androgen exposure — is associated with a behavioural difference, with family environment largely held constant, which is hard for a purely social account to explain.'*
- After Martin & Halverson: *'This is USED as support for the cognitive explanation because the memory distortion shows an internal schema actively filtering information — a mental process, not simply copying a rewarded model.'*

**Why this scores:** each study is now doing argumentative work (Criterion C, 'used'), and the contrast between a hormone-driven vs a cognition-driven mechanism feeds Criterion D. Describing all four studies without linking sentences would leave you knowledge-heavy but research-light — the classic mid-band trap.

sections.content: Social and cultural context, and the role of peers

None of the three explanations operates in a vacuum. Which behaviours count as gender-'appropriate', which models a child sees, and what gets reinforced are all set by the surrounding culture. Cross-cultural variation in gender roles is strong evidence that roles are not fixed by biology alone: what is 'masculine' or 'feminine' differs markedly across societies and eras. Peers matter enormously in childhood and adolescence — peer groups reward conformity to gender norms and sanction deviation, acting as a powerful reinforcement and modelling system beyond the family. This is exactly why a good essay resists single-factor explanations: culture and peers determine the inputs that the cognitive and social mechanisms then process.

items: Culture sets the content of gender roles — hence cross-cultural variation in what counts as 'appropriate'.
Peers reinforce and model gender norms, especially strongly in adolescence.
Media provides symbolic models, extending social learning far beyond the home.
These contextual factors are the inputs that biological predispositions, schemas and reinforcement all act upon.

sections.content: Adolescence and identity formation (Erikson and Marcia, in brief)

Gender identity is consolidated within the broader adolescent task of forming an identity. Erikson framed adolescence as the psychosocial stage of 'identity vs role confusion', in which a coherent sense of self must be built or the person risks confusion about their place in the world. Marcia operationalised this into four statuses along two dimensions — exploration and commitment: diffusion (neither), foreclosure (commitment without exploration), moratorium (active exploration, not yet committed) and achievement (commitment after exploration). This framework is the developmental backdrop: adolescence is when many identity elements, gender among them, are actively questioned and settled.
Evaluation — thinking critically about the explanations and research

items: **Reductionism vs interactionism** — each single explanation is reductionist. Biology cannot explain cultural variation in roles; schema theory does not explain the prenatal hormone effects; social learning struggles with the Imperato-McGinley cases. The mature position is interaction.

**Correlation, not causation** — CAH studies are quasi-experimental: androgen exposure is not manipulated, and CAH girls may also be treated differently, so a purely causal biological reading is unsafe.
**Cultural and temporal bias** — much gender research is Western and dated; findings on 'boys' toys' partly reflect the norms of the sample's time and place, so generalisation is limited.

**Methodological strengths and limits** — Martin & Halverson's memory measure gives clean cognitive evidence but in an artificial task; Fagot's naturalistic observation is high in ecological validity but low in control and open to observer effects.

**Direction of effect** — differential reinforcement (Fagot) could reflect parents responding to pre-existing child differences rather than creating them, complicating the social-learning causal story.

sections.question: Worked example 2 — Turn one study into a critical point, not a summary. Take Fagot (1978). A weak answer writes three sentences summarising it. Show how to convert it into a developed critical-thinking point that would credit Criterion D.
sections.solution:
**Weak (description only):** 'Fagot observed parents and toddlers at home and found parents reinforced gender-appropriate play more than inappropriate play, supporting social learning theory.' → This is fine for Criterion B/C but adds nothing to Criterion D.

**Developed (critical thinking):** 'Fagot's naturalistic observation is high in ecological validity because it captures reinforcement in real family life rather than a laboratory. However, its correlational design cannot establish direction of causation: parents may reinforce gender-typed play, OR they may be responding to differences the child already shows — possibly biological in origin. This ambiguity is itself an argument for an interactionist model, because the same finding is compatible with both the social and biological explanations. It also weakens any claim that reinforcement ALONE creates gender roles.'

**Why this scores:** the developed version evaluates the method (ecological validity vs control), raises the direction-of-causation problem, and then USES that problem to advance the essay's interactionist thesis — three moves that push Criterion D towards the top band instead of leaving the study as inert description.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB markband (out of 22)

sections.question: Discuss the formation and development of gender identity or role. [22]
sections.solution:
**Model essay**

**Thesis (focus):** Gender identity and gender role develop through the interaction of biological, cognitive and social factors, and no single explanation is sufficient. This essay discusses two explanations in depth — the biological (prenatal hormones) and the cognitive (gender-schema theory) — supported by Berenbaum & Hines (1992) and Martin & Halverson (1983), before arguing that the evidence points towards an interactionist account in which biology, cognition and the social/cultural context each contribute.

**Defining the terms.** Gender identity is a person's internal sense of their own gender, whereas gender role is the set of behaviours a society treats as appropriate for that gender. Keeping these distinct matters, because the explanations below speak more directly to gender-typed behaviour (role) while carrying implications for identity.

**Explanation 1 — biological.** The biological explanation argues that prenatal hormones, especially androgens such as testosterone, organise the developing brain and bias later gender-typed behaviour. Berenbaum & Hines (1992) tested this by comparing girls with congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH), who experience high prenatal androgen exposure, with their unaffected female relatives during free play. The CAH girls showed a stronger preference for toys typically chosen by boys. This USES the study as genuine support: because the key difference is a prenatal hormonal one, with much of the family environment shared, the behavioural difference is hard to explain by socialisation alone. Imperato-McGinley's (1974) cases of 5-alpha-reductase deficiency, where children raised as girls adopted a male role at puberty, strengthen the claim that biology can shape gender even against the gender of rearing. However, the biological account is reductionist: it cannot explain why the CONTENT of gender roles varies so much across cultures, and the CAH design is quasi-experimental, so causation cannot be cleanly established — CAH girls may also be treated differently by others.

**Explanation 2 — cognitive (gender-schema theory).** Gender-schema theory (Bem, 1981; Martin & Halverson, 1981) argues that once children can label their own gender they build a schema and then actively attend to, remember and imitate schema-consistent information while filtering out inconsistent information. Martin & Halverson (1983) showed children pictures of gender-consistent and gender-inconsistent activities and tested memory a week later; children remembered consistent images better and distorted inconsistent ones to fit their schema (e.g. recalling a girl, not a boy, cooking). This USES the study to demonstrate an internal cognitive mechanism — the child is not passively copying a rewarded model but actively reshaping information — which the social learning account alone cannot capture. The strength of this evidence is its clean demonstration of schema-driven processing; its limitation is the artificiality of the memory task and the fact that schema theory explains how gender knowledge is organised better than it explains where the initial content comes from.

**Critical discussion — towards interaction.** Placed side by side, the two explanations are complementary rather than rivals. Biology (Berenbaum & Hines) can bias early preferences; cognition (Martin & Halverson) then organises experience around a gender schema; and the social/cultural context supplies the models and reinforcement — evident in Fagot's (1978) finding that parents differentially reinforce gender-typed play, and in the strong cross-cultural variation in what counts as an 'appropriate' role. Each single-factor account is therefore reductionist: biology cannot explain cultural variation, schema theory cannot explain prenatal effects, and social learning struggles with the Imperato-McGinley cases and with the direction-of-causation problem (parents may respond to pre-existing differences rather than create them). Methodologically the evidence is mixed — quasi-experiments and naturalistic observation trade control for validity — so confident single-cause claims are unwarranted.

**Conclusion.** Gender identity and role are best understood as the product of interacting biological, cognitive and social/cultural processes across development, consolidated during the adolescent identity work described by Erikson and Marcia. The supporting studies, used rather than merely described, each illuminate one mechanism while exposing the limits of treating it as the whole story — which is precisely why an interactionist account is the most defensible.

---

**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 2 essay, out of 22)**

The essay is marked on five criteria: A Focus (2), B Knowledge and understanding (6), C Use of research (6), D Critical thinking (6), E Clarity and organisation (2).

- **A — Focus (2/2):** The introduction states an explicit thesis and defines gender identity vs role, and the command term 'discuss' is honoured by weighing explanations rather than describing one. The question is addressed throughout, not just restated — top band.
- **B — Knowledge and understanding (6/6):** Two explanations are explained accurately and in depth (biological mechanism of prenatal androgens; the schema-filtering mechanism), with correct terminology used appropriately. Knowledge is relevant and detailed, not padded — top band.
- **C — Use of research (6/6):** Two studies are present (clearing the one-study cap), each with aim/procedure/findings, and — decisively — each is explicitly USED with a linking sentence that states what it demonstrates about its explanation. Research supports the argument rather than decorating it — top band.
- **D — Critical thinking (6/6):** Critical thinking is DEVELOPED, not listed: reductionism, the correlation/quasi-experiment problem, cultural bias, direction of causation, and method trade-offs are each tied back to the interactionist thesis. The evaluation drives the argument — top band.
- **E — Clarity and organisation (2/2):** A clear thesis-body-conclusion structure, signposted paragraphs, and a conclusion that answers the question — top band.

**Why this reaches the top of each band:** the essay does the two things weak answers skip — it USES its studies with explicit linking sentences (protecting Criterion C) and it DEVELOPS each evaluation point into the argument rather than listing it (protecting Criterion D). Those are the exact moves the marking engine looks for.

sections.content: Press the Practice button to write your own answer to this [22] essay and have our marking engine grade it against the same five criteria above — it tells you your band on each and why. Watch the two caps built into the mark scheme: (1) if you use only ONE study, Criterion C (use of research) is capped at the mid-band no matter how good the study is — so include two, each explicitly used; and (2) if your answer is descriptive only — retelling theories and studies without evaluating — Criterion D (critical thinking) is capped in the bottom band. Beating both caps is usually the difference between a mid-teens and a low-20s mark.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Confusing gender identity with gender role** — identity is the internal felt sense; role is the socially expected behaviour. Blurring them makes studies mismatch the claim and quietly lowers Focus and Knowledge.

**Describing studies instead of USING them** — retelling a procedure earns Knowledge marks but not 'use of research' marks. Add a linking sentence: 'this supports/challenges the explanation because...'.
**Using only one study** — our marking engine caps Criterion C when a single study carries the whole essay. Include two well-explained studies tied to your two explanations.

**Listing evaluation instead of developing it** — a bullet list of 'strengths and limitations' is not critical thinking. Each point must be explained and linked back to the argument to credit Criterion D.
**Single-factor over-claiming** — asserting gender is 'purely biological' or 'purely learned' ignores the cross-cultural and case evidence; the defensible position is interaction.

**Misstating gender-schema theory as imitation** — schema theory is about active mental filtering of information, not simply copying a rewarded model; conflating it with social learning loses accuracy marks.

**Answering a different question** — 'discuss the formation and development of gender' is not the same as 'describe theories of identity'. Keep every paragraph pointed at formation/development of gender identity or role.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 essay marked: 'Discuss the formation and development of gender identity or role. [22]'
